items.title: The biggest problem with solid-state batteries may finally be solved
items.content_text: Researchers solved the mystery of how soft lithium dendrites crack the hard ceramic inside solid-state batteries, triggering short circuits. The breakthrough could help engineers build safer, longer-lasting batteries for smartphones, electric vehicles, and other electronics.

items.title: Simple water trick slashes diesel engine pollution by over 60%
items.content_text: A surprisingly simple fuel modification could help tackle one of diesel engines’ biggest problems: pollution. Researchers reviewing studies from around the world found that mixing small amounts of water into diesel fuel can dramatically reduce harmful emissions, including nitrogen oxides and soot, while maintaining or even improving engine efficiency.

items.title: Chinese sodium battery surprised scientists by matching key Tesla benchmarks
items.content_text: Researchers found that a Chinese sodium-ion battery performs far better than expected, with production quality and design features comparable to Tesla’s batteries. If engineers can improve cold-weather charging and energy density, sodium could become a cheaper and more abundant alternative to lithium for EVs and large-scale energy storage.

items.title: MIT’s new spacecraft engine could send tiny satellites to Mars
items.content_text: MIT researchers have shown that one fuel can power both chemical and electric spacecraft thrusters, potentially transforming what small satellites can do. The approach combines quick bursts of speed with highly efficient long-range propulsion in a single compact system. A NASA-supported CubeSat mission will soon test the technology in orbit.

items.title: New light-powered chip could accelerate AI and quantum computing
items.content_text: Scientists have created a tiny chip that can generate, steer, and read light-based information all in one device, marking a major leap toward ultra-fast, energy-efficient computing. The breakthrough uses atomically thin materials and nanoscale structures to control a unique quantum property of light called the “valley” degree of freedom, allowing information to be encoded in new ways.

items.title: New solar desalination breakthrough makes fresh water without toxic brine
items.content_text: Scientists have developed a solar desalination system that turns seawater into drinking water without creating environmentally damaging brine. Special laser-textured metal panels use sunlight to evaporate water while automatically moving salt deposits away from the working surface, preventing clogging. The process was successfully tested with water from three oceans and can recover nearly all salts as solids. Those leftover materials could even become a source of valuable lithium for batteries.

items.title: Scientists just found a faster, cleaner way to extract lithium for EV batteries
items.content_text: A breakthrough lithium-extraction method could help solve one of clean energy’s dirtiest problems. Researchers at Columbia Engineering have developed a fast new technique that pulls lithium directly from salty underground brines using a temperature-sensitive solvent, avoiding the giant evaporation ponds that can take years and drain precious water supplies. Even better, the method works on low-quality lithium sources that current technologies struggle to use.

items.title: Ordinary WiFi can now identify people with near perfect accuracy
items.content_text: Scientists in Germany have demonstrated a startling new form of surveillance: identifying people using nothing more than ordinary WiFi signals. By analyzing how radio waves bounce around a room, researchers can effectively “see” and recognize individuals — even if they are not carrying a device and even if their phone is turned off.

items.title: NASA just tested a powerful new thruster that could send humans to Mars
items.content_text: A powerful new electromagnetic thruster has taken a major step forward after a successful high-energy test at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory. Fueled by lithium vapor and driven by intense magnetic forces, the experimental engine reached record-breaking power levels—far beyond anything currently used in space. Glowing hotter than molten lava and firing inside a specialized vacuum chamber, the thruster hints at a future where spacecraft could travel farther and more efficiently than ever before.

items.title: Scientists develop dirt-powered fuel cell that could replace batteries
items.content_text: Scientists have developed a fuel cell that uses microbes in soil to produce electricity. The device can power underground sensors for tasks like monitoring moisture or detecting touch, without needing batteries or solar panels. It works in both dry and wet conditions and even lasts longer than similar technologies. This could pave the way for sustainable, low-maintenance sensors in farming and environmental monitoring.

items.title: A vision sensor that programs its own timescales - Nature Sensors
items.content_text: A phototransistor that couples fast charge trapping with slow ferroelectric polarization turns photocarrier lifetime from a fixed material parameter into a programmable hardware variable, enabling one sensor array to adapt its visual memory to motion across three orders of magnitude in speed.

items.title: Unbreakable battery positive electrodes - Nature Nanotechnology
items.content_text: Positive electrode active materials nanocoated with shape-memory polymers show improved electrochemical and mechanical stability during battery operation.

items.title: Electron orbitals choose a direction - Nature Physics
items.content_text: Orbital order is difficult to isolate because it often intertwines with lattice and magnetic transitions. Now, a study on an intermetallic compound reveals a surface 5d-orbital order through its electronic band structure and real-space fingerprints.

items.title: Electronic structure engineering of PVA/ZnO/graphene oxide nanocomposites: a DFT study toward CO₂ and humidity detection - Scientific Reports
A natural solution from caterpillar cuticles for flexible impact-resistant materials - Nature Communications
items.content_text: Here, authors explore the energy dissipation mechanism in the cuticle of Ostrinia furnacalis (Asian corn borer) to identify key structural proteins, FCSP-1 and FCSP-2, which undergo liquid-liquid phase separation to form flexible, impact-resistant materials.

items.title: Teaching a transformer to think like a chemist: predicting nanocluster stability - npj Computational Materials
items.content_text: npj Computational Materials ( 2026 ) Cite this article

items.title: Self-assembly and protein-responsive properties of biotin-conjugated fork-like mesogens at aqueous/liquid-crystalline interfaces - Polymer Journal
items.content_text: Protein-responsive liquid-crystalline (LC) materials are developed by self-assembly of biotin-conjugated fork-like mesogens at aqueous/LC interfaces. Ordering transitions of the LC interfaces in response to avidin are induced by mixing the biotin-conjugated and biotin-free fork-like mesogens, which leads to changes in optical appearance. The spacer lengths of the biotin-conjugated fork-like mesogens also affect the protein-responsive properties. The results of the present study may provide the design of aquatic functional LC materials with tunable protein responsiveness.

items.title: Sustainable valorization of organic solid wastes: macromolecular humic acid-stabilized cinnamon essential oil emulsions with enhanced antibacterial properties - Polymer Journal
items.content_text: Humic acid extracted from composted tangerine meat, Chinese medicine residues, and soy sauce residues serves as an excellent natural emulsifier. It encapsulates cinnamon oil into a stable emulsion and exhibits superior storage stability. Crucially, the emulsion effectively inhibited bacteria, such as E. coli, revealing a green strategy for waste valorization.
